games.title: Bog Standard?
games.objective: Analyze the changes in Grismoor Mire's ecological variables — rainfall, water-table depth, sphagnum cover and bog area.
games.study_md: Grismoor Mire is a raised peat bog. Peat depth averages 6 m, crossed by 2.4 km of boardwalk and patrolled by 7 rangers. Declining metrics: bog area 720 ha now, down from 900 ha ten years ago; sphagnum cover 45%, down from 60% ten years ago; dragonfly species also falling. Water-table depth is logged at two microsites — a wet Hollow and a dry Hummock.
distractors: 2.4 km** of boardwalk ⟨distractor⟩ and patrolled by **7 rangers

exhibits.title: Monthly rainfall: rainy days / total rainfall (mm)

analysis.prompt: Difference in rainy days between the month with the highest total rainfall and the lowest. → Feb (140 mm, 13 days) − May (12 mm, 3 days). picks months by rainy-days (Jan 14 − May 3)
How much higher is the Hollow's water-table range vs the Hummock's? → Hollow 30−16=14; Hummock 15−7=8; (14−8)/8. divides by the larger range (14)
% reductions over 10 years: sphagnum (60→45); bog area (900→720). uses the current value as base
Month with the highest %-difference (Hollow−Hummock)/Hummock: Dec 150 · Jan 200.00% · Feb 100 · Mar 81.82 · Apr 86.67. ranks by absolute gap (Dec 18 cm)

games.title: Otter Chaos
games.objective: Analyze the ecological and economic impact of an otter-restocking program on Sorrel Cove — its effect on the sea-urchin population, kelp recovery and dive-tourism revenue.
games.study_md: Sorrel Cove is a temperate kelp forest 9 km² in area with 12 dive operators. An exploding sea-urchin population has grazed the kelp into a barren. At Year 1 the cove holds 150,000 urchins, growing 10%/year unchecked. The program restocks 50 sea otters, each removing 400 urchins/year (20,000/year total), monitored over 5 years. The kelp can sustain at most 175,000 urchins. Kelp cover is 45% in Year 1; under restocking it recovers, without it falls 3 pp/year. Each +1% kelp supports +500 reef-fish index and +$5,000 dive-tourism revenue.
distractors: 9 km²** in area ⟨distractor⟩ with **12 dive operators

exhibits.title: Sea-urchin population: restock vs no intervention
Kelp recovery and dive-tourism revenue (under restock)

analysis.prompt: (a) Urchins removed per year → 50 × 400. (b) Earliest year the restocked population falls to ≤ 120,000 [Year 5 / Year 6 / Year 7] − 20,000 ≤ 120,000; Y5 = 126,795 > 120,000). (reads 126,795 as "≈120k")

To hit a 100,000 target immediately at Year 5, what % of the Year-5 population must be removed? → (126,795 − 100,000)/126,795. divides the gap by the 100,000 target instead of the current Year-5 population
(a) Total kelp recovery over 5 years. (b) Reef-fish index at Year 5 (base 30,000, +500/pp) = 30,000 + 14×500. (uses the Year-5 Δ of 5 pp, not cumulative 14)
Without restocking, kelp falls 3 pp/yr. (a) Cover at Year 5 = 45 − 3×4. (b) Revenue foregone vs the 45% baseline = (45−33)×$5,000. (counts 5 declines, not 4)

Multi-criteria selection — pick the removal method minimizing $/1,000 removed with bycatch ≤ 5: Diving $4,200 (bycatch 2) · Trapping $3,000 (bycatch 8) · Quicklime $3,600 (bycatch 4). ($3,600). ($3,000, but bycatch 8 > 5)

games.study_md: Merrat is a mineral-rich republic; average temperature 24 °C, elevation 900 m. Prong 1 — reclamation nursery: 30 hectares × 6,000 plants/ha (180,000 plants), mix A 45% / B 35% / C 20%; nutrient uptake A 3 · B 2 · C 6 kg/plant/yr. Prong 2 — export revenue + reinvestment: cobalt sells at a steady $300/tonne; from Year 5 the state channels 30% of export revenue into the reclamation fund; it also issues reclamation credits per unit of soil-impact offset.

games.study_md: Salvench is a subscription streaming service in 3 markets with a 12-person support team. It began the year with 50,000 subscribers, added 12,000 gross new subscribers, and lost 20% of the starting base to churn. ARPU is $360/year; gross margin 70%; customer-acquisition cost (CAC) $90.

analysis.prompt: Net subscriber growth this year → gross adds 12,000 − churned (ending base 52,000). (ending 62,000 — counts gross adds, ignores churn)
Ending annual recurring revenue (ARR) → 52,000 × $360. (uses the naive 62,000 base)
Lifetime value (LTV), margin-adjusted → (ARPU × gross margin)/churn = /0.20 = $252/0.20. (ARPU/churn, ignores that only the margin is profit)
LTV : CAC → 1,260/90. (uses the $1,800 gross LTV)

cases.prompt: Churned subscribers — 50,000 × 20%. (assumes the base is sticky)
ARR growth rate — net 2,000/50,000. (gross 12,000/50,000)
Average customer lifetime — 1/churn = 1/0.20. (ignores churn)
CAC payback — $90 ÷
Gross adds to grow 10% — to grow the base 10% (+5,000 net) you must also replace 10,000 churn gross adds. (forgets churn must be backfilled)
Net revenue movement — ARR gained from adds − ARR lost to churn = (12,000−10,000)×$360. (counts only the 12,000 adds' revenue)

games.title: Fishing for Trouble
games.objective: Analyze the sustainable harvest of the Braelin Fen fishery under logistic growth.
games.study_md: Braelin Fen is a lake fishery of 420 ha with 7 licensed boats. The fish stock stands at N = 60,000 tonnes against a carrying capacity K = 100,000, with intrinsic growth rate r = 40%/year. Annual surplus production (the harvestable growth) follows P(N) = r × N × (1 − N/K); a harvest equal to the surplus keeps the stock stable.

analysis.prompt: Sustainable quota at the current stock → 0.4 × 60,000 × (1 − 0.6) = 0.4 × 60,000 × 0.4. (0.4 × 60,000 — applies the growth rate to the whole stock, dropping the density term (1 − N/K))
If the naive quota of 24,000 t is taken, next-year stock → 60,000 + 9,600 − 24,000 (it falls). (believes 24,000 is sustainable)
Maximum sustainable yield (MSY) → r × K / 4 = 0.4 × 100,000 / 4 . (0.4 × 50,000, again dropping the density term)
Stock level that maximizes sustainable yield → K/2. (assumes the fullest stock yields the most)

cases.prompt: Surplus at half-capacity — P(50,000) = 0.4 × 50,000 × 0.5 (the peak). (0.4 × 50,000)
Complete the table — surplus at N=80,000 → 0.4 × 80,000 × (1 − 0.8) = 0.4 × 80,000 × 0.2. (linear-interpolates 9,600↔? / applies flat rate proportionally)
Overharvest gap — naive quota 24,000 − sustainable 9,600 taken beyond replacement. (sees no overharvest)
Sustainable harvest rate — sustainable quota ÷ current stock = 9,600/60,000. (equates the sustainable rate with the growth rate r)
Rebuild — with zero harvest, next-year stock → 60,000 + 9,600 (recovers toward K). (thinks a fished-down stock is static)
Pick the quota — A 8,000 t (under) · B 9,600 t · C 12,000 t (over). (picks the highest tonnage this year, ignoring next-year collapse)

games.title: The Long Game
games.objective: Analyze a cull of the Nerreth Reach deer herd, accounting for the delayed maturation of juveniles.
games.study_md: Nerreth Reach is an upland estate of 4,800 ha with 5 stalking beats. The adult deer herd stands at 100,000 in Year 1. A cull removes 40,000 adults/year from Year 2. Twist fact (recruitment lag): juveniles take 2 years to mature into the adult herd; each year the maturing cohort adds recruits equal to 30% of the adult herd two years earlier. (Before the cull the herd was stable at 100,000, so the Year-2 and Year-3 recruits come from the large pre-cull population.)

exhibits.title: Adult herd: naive (no replacement) vs realized
Maturing-cohort recruits (2-yr lag = 30% of adults two years prior)

analysis.prompt: (a) Adults removed per year. (b) Earliest year the herd falls to ≤ 50,000 [Year 3 / Year 5 / Year 6]? - *Step 1 (naive — cull only):* 100,000 − 40,000×(t−1) → Y2 60,000 · Y3 20,000 → crosses at Year 3. This is the naive path . - *Step 2 (decompose — Exhibit 2):* each year adds recruits = 30% of adults two years prior. Realized = prev − 40,000 + recruits: Y4 = 80,000 − 40,000 + 0.3×90,000 · Y5 = 67,000 − 40,000 + 0.3×80,000 · Y6 = 51,000 − 40,000 + 0.3×67,000. - Answer: Year 6 . (ignores the maturing cohort — early recruits (30,000) mask the decline, so the herd holds far longer)
From the realized Year-6 herd (31,100), what further % cut hits a 25,000 target? → (31,100 − 25,000)/31,100. (÷ 25,000 target)
Heath recovers as the herd falls: Δcover +2/+3/+4/+4/+5 pp over Years 2–6. (a) Total recovery. (b) Grouse index (base 20,000, +400/pp) = 20,000 + 18×400. (uses the Year-6 Δ of 5 pp)
Without the cull, heath cover falls 3 pp/yr from 55%. (a) Year 6 = 55 − 3×5. (b) Revenue foregone vs 55% baseline = (55−40)×$6,000. (6 declines)

cases.prompt: Carrying capacity — the estate sustains ≤ 120,000 adults; the pre-cull herd (stable 100,000) never exceeds it. (reads the recruit inflows as unchecked growth)
Complete the table — recruits Y4 = 30% × adults. (repeats the flat early figure, ignoring that adults two years prior have fallen)
Multi-criteria selection — min $/1,000 removed with welfare-score ≤ 5: Stalking $4,500 (score 2) · Contraception $3,000 (score 8) · Fencing $3,800 (score 4).. ($3,000, score 8 > 5)
Max %-decline by beat — adult density: North 55,000→44,000 · Mid 20,000→13,000 · Glen 25,000→20,750 . Largest by %. (abs −11,000). Gap 15 pp
Priority index — |%-decline| + regeneration gain: North 24 · Mid 40 · Glen 45. (35)
Bounty payout (cascade) — bounty $50 per adult removed, Years 2–6. Removals are the cull , not the net herd change

games.title: Value Added?
games.objective: Analyze whether Ombria's new ore refinery raises the republic's export economy, and its restoration fund.
games.study_md: Ombria is an ore-exporting republic; plateau 1,400 m, 6 mining concessions. Prong 1 — restoration nursery: 40 ha × 6,000 plants/ha (240,000 plants), mix A 35% / B 40% / C 25%; uptake A 5 · B 3 · C 4 kg/plant/yr. Prong 2: Ombria mines 500,000 t of ore/year, historically all exported raw at $80/t. It has built a smelter that consumes 300,000 t of ore to produce 100,000 t of refined metal (a 3:1 feed ratio), sold at $300/t refined. From Year 5 the state reinvests 25% of realized export revenue.

exhibits.title: Export revenue: before vs after the refinery

analysis.prompt: Nursery uptake/year → A 84,000×5=420,000 · B 96,000×3=288,000 · C 60,000×4=240,000. (simple average of rates 4.0)

By how much does the refinery raise total export revenue? - *Step 1 (headline):* the refinery exports 100,000 t × $300 of refined metal — apparently $30M of new revenue. - *Step 2 (decompose — unprompted):* the smelter consumed 300,000 t of ore that used to be exported raw at $80. Total export revenue is now $16M raw + $30M refined, up from $40M. - Answer: +$6M (the $30M refined minus the $24M raw the refinery cannibalized). (counts the refined revenue as pure new, ignoring the ore it ate)
What is Ombria's new total export revenue? → $16M raw + $30M refined. ($40M raw unchanged + $30M refined — double-counts the 300,000 t of ore)
Royalty at 12% of realized total exports → 0.12 × $46M

cases.prompt: Royalty on net — 12% × realized total $46M
Break-even ore price — at what raw price does the refinery add nothing? refined $30M = 300,000 t × P → P. Above $100/t, refining the ore destroys value
Value-add per tonne of ore fed — refined value $30M ÷ 300,000 t = $100/t vs the $80/t raw price net uplift. (the refined price, ignoring the ore given up)
Refining cost — if smelting costs $50/t refined ($5M), the net gain → $6M − $5M. (still the gross refined revenue)
Reinvestment fund — 25% of realized total $46M

Ore allocation — with 500,000 t of ore, feeding the smelter (3:1) needs 300,000 t for 100,000 t refined; the max refined output if all ore is smelted = 500,000/3. (treats one tonne of ore as one tonne of refined)

games.title: A Fine Balance
games.objective: Analyze the reach, health impact, cost and payback of Sabelle District's rural sanitation program.
games.study_md: Sabelle District has 80,000 households across 7 sub-counties, average household size 5.0 people (400,000 people). A subsidised latrine program cuts diarrhoeal disease; pilot data suggests 70% adoption. Each adopting household avoids 6 disease cases/year. Latrines cost $30/household; the program carries $400,000 fixed cost; each case avoided saves the health system $8.

analysis.prompt: Households covered → 80,000 × 70%. (all households)
Cases avoided/year → covered households × 6 = 56,000 × 6. (multiplies by people: 56,000 × 5.0 × 6 — the effect is stated per household, not per person; household size is a distractor)
First-year cost → 56,000 × $30 + $400,000. (omits fixed)
(a) Annual saving → 336,000 × $8. (b) Payback → 2,080,000 / 2,688,000. (uses the per-person 1,680,000 cases)

cases.prompt: Coverage gap — 80,000 − 56,000
Cost per outcome — $2,080,000 / 336,000. (÷ the per-person 1,680,000 cases — understates by counting people as cases)
Per-household vs per-capita — the program prevents 6 cases per household, so a village of 400 households avoids 400 × 6. (400 × 5 people × 6)
Multi-region allocation — best cases per $: N 120,000/$2.4M=50,000 · M 90,000/$1.5M=60,000 · S 80,000/$1.2M=66,667. (most total, 120,000)
Incremental scale-up — 70%→90%: 0.20×80,000×$30

Break-even adoption [9b-model] — save(a)=a×80,000×6×$8; cost(a)=a×80,000×$30+$400,000. a×(3,840,000−2,400,000)=400,000 → a. (fixed ÷ full-adoption saving). Dedup vs Nyala/Dombe/Kavango/Lenga/Tesvan: 80k hh · size 5.0 · 70% · 6 cases · $30 · $400k · $8 saving · **

games.title: No Catch
games.objective: Decide which of Sennik District's two zones to fund first for its clean-water program — and validate that no hidden reversal changes the answer.
games.study_md: Sennik District must fund one of two zones first. North Zone: 80,000 waterborne-illness cases avoided/year, program cost $2.0M, 16,000 households. South Zone: 50,000 cases avoided/year, cost $1.6M, 12,500 households. A shared district overhead of $600,000 ⟨may or may not matter⟩ could be added to either. Average household size 4.8.
distractors: North Zone:** 80,000 waterborne-illness cases avoided/year, program cost **$2.0M**, **16,000 households**. **South Zone:** 50,000 cases avoided/year, cost **$1.6M**, **12,500 households**. A shared district overhead of **$600,000** ⟨may or may not matter⟩ could be added to either. Average household size **4.8

analysis.prompt: Cost per case → North $2.0M/80,000; South $1.6M/50,000. North is cheaper per case and avoids more total cases

Which zone to fund first? North — it wins on both total cases (80,000 > 50,000) and cost-effectiveness ($25 < $32). — the naive "most total cases" pick and the cost-effectiveness pick agree; there is no reversal.* (This is a Clean case: the trap in prior programs — where cost-per-outcome flips the "most total" pick — does not** fire here. The candidate must check for it and say so.)
Does adding the $600,000 overhead flip it? North $2.6M/80,000; South $2.2M/50,000 → still North. No flip
Cases per household → North 80,000/16,000; South 50,000/12,500 → still North. No flip

cases.prompt: Cost per case — North $25.00 vs South $32.00 → North. *No cheaper-total-loser reversal.*
Cases per $M — North 40,000 vs South 31,250 → North. *Cost-effectiveness agrees with total volume.*
With shared overhead — North $32.50 vs South $44.00 → North holds. *Fixed-cost reversal closed.*
Per household — North 5.00 vs South 4.00 → North holds. *Per-household reversal closed.*

Sensitivity (most-sensitive hypothesis) — if North's cases were 20% lower (64,000), cost per case = $2.0M/64,000, still below South's $32.00 → North holds. This is the tightest check and it still favours North

Verdict — name every reversal hypothesis ; all close to North. Recommend North and state explicitly that no reversal exists. (picks South "because it's cheaper in total dollars," a reversal the numbers do not support)

games.title: Overlapping Interests
games.objective: Analyze the designation composition of the Bramwold Range, where some ground carries two designations at once.
games.study_md: The Bramwold Range covers 6,000 km² total, crossed by 5 bridleways and 2 visitor centres. Three designations overlap on the map: Conservation 3,000 km², Access 2,000 km², Grazing 1,500 km² — and 800 km² carry both Conservation and Access at once. Grazing does not overlap the others.

exhibits.title: Designation map of the Bramwold Range

analysis.prompt: What % of the range carries at least one designation? → union = 3,000 + 2,000 − 800 + 1,500 = 5,700 → 5,700/6,000
Area that is both Conservation and Access = 800/6,000 of the range
Conservation-only (not also Access) → 3,000 − 800 = 2,200 → 2,200/6,000. (3,000/6,000 — ignores the shared 800)
Undesignated ground → 6,000 − 5,700 = 300 → 300/6,000. (thinks the summed designations already fill the range)

cases.prompt: Double-count magnitude — the naive sum (6,500) exceeds the true designated area (5,700) by 800 km² — exactly the overlap counted twice. (sees no double-count)

Composition count×% — most expected at-risk parcels ? | Zone | Conservation | Access | Grazing | Overlap | Undesig. | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Parcels | 30 | 26 | 40 | 12 | 10 | | At-risk % | 18% | 20% | 16% | 40% | 30% | → Conservation 5.4 · Access 5.2 · Grazing 6.4 · Overlap 4.8 · Undesig. 3.0
Ratio — Conservation : Grazing = 3,000 : 1,500. (unsimplified)
Reclassification — if the 800 km² overlap is reassigned to Conservation-only (Access releases it), Access's share of the range → (2,000 − 800)/6,000. (uses the pre-reclass 2,000)
Sum-to-total check — Conservation-only 2,200 + Access-only 1,200 + overlap 800 + Grazing 1,500 + undesignated 300 (reconciles to the range). (double-counts the overlap, overshooting the range)
